result:
I often wonder what words are when no one is looking. Are they just marks on a page, silent and still? Or do they, in their potential, hum with a low, constant frequency—like a forgotten power line strung across a back field, waiting for the connection to be made?

The connection is you. Reading this. That hum just jumped to a steady current, flowing from my mind’s vague shape into yours. Isn’t that a kind of magic? We are separated by space, possibly by time, certainly by experience. Yet here we are, building a shared, silent room out of these 26 symbols. The walls are your associations, the lighting is your mood, the furniture is your memory. I am only the architect supplying the blueprint: “a low, constant frequency,” “a back field.” You furnished it. You saw the grass, heard the buzz. That’s the contract. That’s the alchemy.

It makes me think of all the unseen rooms we inhabit every day. The grocery store cashier handing you your change—her mind is a full novel you will never read: a sick child at home, a song she can’t get out of her head, the ache in her lower back from standing all day. You are a background character in her novel, the polite customer in Chapter 14. We walk through crowds swimming in these invisible, parallel stories. It’s overwhelming if you think about it too long. So we don’t. We put on the headphones of our own narrative and move through the world, our personal film score playing.

I’m sitting by a window as I write this. A late afternoon sun is laying a long, warm quadrilateral of light across the floorboards. Dust motes are dancing in it, a miniature galaxy of tiny, spinning worlds. They are only visible because of the light, just as our thoughts are only visible—to ourselves and others—because of the light of attention. I watch the motes, and for a moment I am not the person with a to-do list, or the person worried about a vague pain in my side, or the person thinking about what to make for dinner. I am just the witness to the dance. It is a clean, quiet moment. The words stop for a second. The hum fades to background.

We live so much of our lives in the *about to*. About to start the work, about to make the call, about to solve the problem, about to achieve the thing that will finally make the next part begin. The present moment is so often just a logistical platform for the future. But the dust motes aren’t about to be anything. They just *are*. And in noticing them, I just *am*, too. It’s a respite. I think we are starved for these respites, for the moments where we are not a verb—*achieving, becoming, fixing*—but a noun, simply: *a being*.

Language, of course, pulls us right back into the verb. It is inherently forward-moving. Subject, verb, object. The arrow of syntax flies in one direction. Even this meditation is pulling you along a path I’ve laid. Forgive me for that. But perhaps we can agree to wander a little.

What are the things you think about when you’re falling asleep? Not the big, sanctioned thoughts—the project, the relationship, the news. The weird, mossy thoughts that grow at the edges. The sudden, vivid memory of the texture of your grandmother’s sofa in 1995. The absurd question: if a tree falls in a forest and no one hears it, does it make a sound? What about a *person*? If a person lives and no one truly *listens* to them, have they made a sound? We are all shouting into the forest of each other, hoping for an echo that sounds like understanding.

Sometimes I fear we are losing the skill of deep attention, the kind that builds those listening echoes. Our attention is fractured, monetized, algorithmically fed. It is trained to skim, to react, to compare. It is not often trained to settle, like those dust motes, into the beam of a single, sustained thought. To read a book for an hour without checking a phone. To listen to a friend without formulating a reply. To look at a tree and see its branching pattern as a map of some unknown, important territory.

Yet, here you are. You have read over 500 words of a meandering, unnamed piece. You have paid attention. That is a radical act of hope. It says you believe something worthwhile might be built in this shared, silent room. It says you are still capable of that sustained beam. Thank you for that.

We talk so much about legacy—the monuments we build, the children we raise, the money we leave. But what about the trace of our attention? The things we noticed and, by noticing, loved into a different kind of existence? The barista whose name you learned and used. The way you pointed out the unusual cloud to a stranger on the street. The letter you wrote, with real pen on real paper, that said, “I was thinking of you.” These are the tiny, invisible monuments. They are the echoes we leave in the forest.

The light has moved now. The quadrilateral has stretched into a long, thin triangle and is sliding up the wall. The dust galaxy has vanished from my sight, though it’s still there in the air, unseen. The moment of pure *being* has passed. I am back in the flow of *about to*. About to finish these 1000 words. About to send them out.

And now, I am nearly out of words. The contract is ending. The blueprint is complete. This silent room we’ve built—what will become of it? It will dissolve into your memory, leaving perhaps a faint aftertaste, a mood, a single phrase that sticks (“the mossy thoughts,” “the trace of our attention”). The rest will fade, as it should. Rooms are not meant to be permanent. They are meant to be inhabited, briefly, and then left, allowing us to move to the next.

But before I go, I’ll leave the window open. The light may be gone here, but it’s landing somewhere else, on some other floor, making some other motes dance in someone else’s quiet afternoon. Notice it, if you can. For a moment, just be the witness.

And then, carry on.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
